items.q: Why does a metal spoon in hot water heat up faster than a wooden one?
items.correct: Metals have higher thermal conductivity, so heat is transferred more rapidly through them.
items.misconception: Metal heats up faster because it has a lower specific heat capacity, requiring less energy per degree.
items.random_wrong: Metal heats up faster because it has more thermal energy to begin with.
items.why: Students confuse the rate of heat transfer (conductivity) with how much energy a material can hold (heat capacity).

items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: In a collision between two objects, what quantity is always conserved (in a closed system)?
items.correct: Total momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.
items.misconception: Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions because energy is conserved.
items.random_wrong: Velocity is always conserved in any collision.
items.why: Students conflate momentum conservation with energy conservation; they overlook that energy can become heat/sound in inelastic collisions.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: It's 8 N. “2 N” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: Correct answer: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: Exact answer: 49 N. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
